evidence_quotes.quote: videogame companies reserve the right to render the videogames they've sold to consumers completely inaccessible and unplayable, for any or no reason, at any time.

The videogame industry should at the very least provide tools for their customers to still be able to access these games, or give them the option to host the services required to keep them operational.

paragraphs.text: As an Italian citizen, I believe this is a fantastic proposal that should be expanded to cover the destruction of videogames that has been an issue for consumers for the past 15 years. As it stands videogame companies reserve the right to render the videogames they've sold to consumers completely inaccessible and unplayable, for any or no reason, at any time. This represents a massive breach of consumer rights - something that would be unacceptable for any other category of goods. Yet, the video game industry has operated this way for years without consequence, resulting in hundreds upon hundreds of titles being effectively stripped from the very consumers who paid for them. The videogame industry should at the very least provide tools for their customers to still be able to access these games, or give them the option to host the services required to keep them operational. The Digital Fairness Act, along with the Citizens' Initiatives, stands as a major point of pride for EU citizens: a shining example of a democracy that empowers individuals to have a say in a wide range of issues. I sincerely hope that our voices will finally be heard and that meaningful action will be taken to address this situation, safeguarding the consumer rights of all EU nationals.
